hero.tagline: Mississippi's Justice Courts handle claims up to $3,500 with a straightforward process designed for self-representation.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Appeal to County or Circuit Court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $3,500, you must either waive the excess amount to file in Justice Court or file in a higher court. Splitting one claim into multiple cases to fit under the cap is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Mississippi allows 3 years for oral contracts and 6 years for written contracts to be enforced in small claims court.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $3,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $2,000, and they failed to repay you as promised.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned $1,500 to a colleague who has not repaid it despite multiple reminders.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Document the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $3,500 and failed to make any payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of non-payment.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $2,000 worth of consulting services, and the client has not paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $3,200 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in unpaid rent over the past three months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $1,500 check that was returned for insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Provide the bounced check, bank notice, and any communication with the issuer.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $3,500 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the loan agreement and records of payments made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Mississippi provides avenues for consumers to seek redress for defective products and services, with a 3-year statute of limitations for most claims.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $2,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $1,500 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and evidence of substandard work.

claims.name: Overcharged or faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $3,000 for repairs that did not fix the issue and caused additional damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $3,500 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Present the contract, payment records, and evidence of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods/services
claims.example: You paid $2,500 for catering services, but the vendor did not show up on the event day.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Provide the contract, payment proof, and any communication with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month subscription, but the company continued to charge your account for six months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Bring cancellation confirmation and bank statements showing continued charges.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $3,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Provide the bill of sale, any warranties, and evidence of the defect.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $1,000 with features it did not have, leading you to purchase under false pretenses.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Bring the advertisement, purchase receipt, and evidence of the misrepresentation.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Mississippi allows 3 years to file claims for property damage, including vehicle collisions and damage caused by neighbors.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,000 in damages, and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Provide the police report, repair estimates, and any communication with the other driver.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,500 in damages, and they refuse to cover the costs.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Bring photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $3,500 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Provide the lease agreement, move-in/move-out inspection reports, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,500 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Bring photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your $3,000 bicycle with a friend, and they returned it with significant damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide evidence of the property's condition before and after, and any communication with the custodian.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Mississippi provides a 3-year statute of limitations for most landlord-tenant monetary disputes, excluding eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Security deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, and the landlord refused to address them, forcing you to move out.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Bring photos of the conditions, repair requests, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your $2,000 worth of personal belongings without legal justification.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Provide evidence of the retained property and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your rental unit.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Bring the lease agreement, evidence of the lock change, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid the last month's rent in advance, but the lease ended early, and the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement, payment records, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,500 in unpaid rent over the past four months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent without proper notice
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater and deducted $500 from your rent without notifying the landlord.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair. Provide repair receipts, photos of the issue, and any communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wages
claim_categories.blurb: Mississippi enforces a 3-year statute of limitations for wage-related claims, including unpaid wages and overtime.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $3,000 for hours worked but not compensated.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from non-payment. Bring pay stubs, timesheets, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $2,500.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from non-payment. Provide resignation notice, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,500 in sales commissions that your employer has not paid.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from non-payment. Bring commission agreements, sales records, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer did not pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 20 hours of overtime but were only paid your regular hourly rate.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from non-payment. Provide timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Mississippi allows 3 years to file claims for personal injuries and property disputes.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped and fell in a store, resulting in $3,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from injury date. Bring medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $2,500 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and any communication with the borrower.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $3,500 lawnmower without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from theft date. Bring proof of ownership, police reports, and any communication with the neighbor.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Mississippi provides avenues for resolving disputes related to vehicle repairs and wrongful towing within a 3-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged $3,500 for repairs that did not fix the issue and caused additional damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you incurred $1,500 in fees to retrieve it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from towing date. Provide photos of the parking spot, towing receipts, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled in Justice Court but may require specific procedures beyond small claims.
Justice Courts do not have jurisdiction over disputes involving the title to real property.
Defamation cases typically exceed the monetary limit and involve complex legal issues unsuitable for small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims involve complex legal standards and typically exceed the small claims monetary limit.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are outside the jurisdiction of Justice Courts.
Justice Courts do not have jurisdiction over claims against federal entities.
Workers' compensation claims are handled by the Mississippi Workers' Compensation Commission.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural scope of small claims court.
Justice Courts can only award monetary damages and cannot issue injunctions or orders for specific performance.
Bankruptcy matters are exclusively handled by federal bankruptcy courts.

damages.punitive_explanation: Justice Courts can only award compensatory damages up to $3,500 and do not have the authority to award punitive damages.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in cases where the underlying statute provides for such recovery, such as certain consumer protection claims.

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong county can result in dismissal or transfer, causing delays and additional fees.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing varies by county; contact the local Justice Court clerk for information.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; confirm with the local court.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the statute of limitations when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of fraudulent concealment.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but documenting requests for payment is advisable.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide a written itemization of damages within 45 days of lease termination when withholding a security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements, but documenting complaints and demands can strengthen your case.

forms.name: Statement of Claim
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and the relief sought.

forms.name: Affidavit of Substantial Hardship
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of the intent to appeal a judgment.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: Summons and complaint sent via certified mail with return receipt requested.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Mississippi's long-arm statute and the laws of the state where the defendant resides.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located after diligent efforts, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document all attempts and seek court permission for alternative service methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the scheduled hearing.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even in default, the plaintiff must present evidence to substantiate the claim before a judgment is entered.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to vacate must be filed within 10 days of the judgment; if denied, an appeal can be filed within 10 days of the denial.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to do so.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case, and the judge may ask questions.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide assistance upon request.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 10 days

appeals.type_notes: The appeal results in a new trial in the County or Circuit Court.
appeals.bond_amount_notes: The bond amount is set by the court and typically covers the judgment amount and costs.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 10 days; alternatively, a motion to set aside can be filed.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve it on the debtor's bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; may be limited by exemptions.
exemptions: $10,000 in personal property, including bank accounts

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county clerk to create a lien on the debtor's property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; may take time to realize payment.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, and the sheriff seizes and sells the debtor's property at auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property; may be time-consuming.
exemptions: Various personal property exemptions under state law

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 7-year period expires; renewal extends the judgment for another 7 years.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may represent themselves in Justice Court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must file a statement with the Secretary of State.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: A sole proprietor may authorize an employee to represent the business in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from enforcing contracts in court.

pitfalls.title: Wrong defendant naming
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue 'John's Garage' but the legal entity is 'JG Auto Repair LLC', the judgment may be unenforceable against the LLC's assets.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Search the Secretary of State business records before filing and use the exact registered name; for sole proprietors, use the individual's full legal name.

pitfalls.title: Missed statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing one day after the deadline gets the case dismissed with prejudice.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check the statute of limitations for your specific claim type before filing; for fraud, the discovery rule applies but be prepared to prove when you reasonably could have discovered it.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant is not properly served, the case may be dismissed or delayed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service carefully, and consider using the sheriff or a professional process server.

pitfalls.title: Filing in the wrong venue
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing in the wrong county can result in dismissal or transfer, causing delays and additional fees.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: File in the county where the defendant resides or where the cause of action arose.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; you may need to take additional steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with collection methods like garnishment and liens, and be prepared to pursue them if necessary.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking exemptions in collection
pitfalls.what_happens: Certain debtor assets are exempt from collection, which can limit your ability to satisfy the judgment.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Understand the exemptions under Mississippi law to assess the feasibility of collection efforts.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Justice Court; the process is designed for self-representation, but you may hire one if you choose.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The typical timeline from filing to hearing is 30 to 90 days, depending on the court's schedule and the complexity of the case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Mississippi Justice Court is $3,500.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor, but you must still present evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a judgment to the County or Circuit Court within 10 days of the decision.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Mississippi is valid for 7 years and can be renewed before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Mississippi allows for jury trials in Justice Court if requested by either party.
Eviction cases are handled in Justice Court but may require specific procedures beyond small claims.
The statute of limitations for written contracts is 6 years, while for oral contracts, it is 3 years.
Mississippi's homestead exemption protects up to $75,000 of a debtor's primary residence from creditors.
Justice Courts do not have jurisdiction over disputes involving the title to real property.

interesting_findings: Mississippi's Justice Courts provide a relatively informal setting, making it accessible for individuals without legal representation.
The state allows for the recovery of attorney fees in certain consumer protection cases, providing additional leverage for plaintiffs.
Mississippi's post-judgment interest rate is set at 8%, which can significantly increase the amount owed over time.
